schools.summary: The historical parallel and warning: 'but there were false prophets also among the people (Israel), even as there shall be false teachers among you, who privily shall bring in (pareisaxousin — smuggle in secretly, stealthily) damnable (apoleias — destructive, leading to ruin) heresies (haireseis — factious false doctrines), even denying the Lord (despoten — Master, absolute owner) that bought (agorasanta — purchased, redeemed) them, and bring upon themselves swift destruction' — as Israel had false prophets, so the church will have false teachers (the certainty of their rising), who covertly introduce ruinous false teaching and bring sudden ruin on themselves. On 'the Lord that bought them,' Ellicott (with Wesley, Barnes) takes it as conclusive against partial redemption — Christ redeemed even these who perish; Robertson notes 'these were professing Christians.'

Wesley and Clarke: 'the Lord that bought them' with his own blood — yet these very men perish everlastingly; therefore Christ bought even them that perish, proof that the redemption is universal and not confined to the elect; the false teachers, by their doctrine and works, deny the very Master who purchased them, and so bring on themselves swift destruction.

Gill (and the Calvinist tradition): 'bought' refers to their profession or to a purchase from temporal ruin/bondage — they were professedly redeemed, or the language describes them as they claimed to be (the Master they owned in profession), not that they were truly and effectually redeemed by Christ's atoning blood (since the truly redeemed, the elect, cannot finally perish); the verse describes apostate professors, not those for whom Christ's redemption was effectual.

debates.question: Does 'the Lord that bought them' mean Christ died even for these who perish?

sides.position: Yes — Christ redeemed even those who perish; proof against limited atonement (universal redemption)
No — 'bought' is a professed or temporal purchase, not the effectual redemption of the elect (limited atonement)

themes: there shall be false teachers among you, who privily shall bring in damnable heresies
even denying the Lord that bought them, and bring upon themselves swift destruction
false teachers denying the Lord that bought them
the atonement-extent crux

schools.summary: The success of the error: 'and many shall follow their pernicious ways (aselgeiais — their licentious conduct, lasciviousness, destructive courses); by reason of whom the way of truth shall be evil spoken of (blasphemethesetai — blasphemed, slandered)' — the false teachers not lacking followers (many drawn into their corrupt and ruinous, immoral, sensual ways, their teaching issuing in loose living which many imitate); and because these corrupt teachers and their followers bear the Christian name, the way of truth (the gospel) brought into disrepute, outsiders blaspheming Christianity because of the scandalous lives of these professors (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: many shall follow their pernicious ways
by reason of whom the way of truth shall be evil spoken of
many follow the false teachers, bringing the truth into disrepute
the gospel blasphemed through scandalous lives

schools.summary: The motive and doom: 'and through covetousness (pleonexia — greed for gain) shall they with feigned (plastois — fabricated, counterfeit, manufactured) words make merchandise of (emporeusontai — exploit, trade upon) you: whose judgment now of a long time lingereth not (does not delay, is not idle), and their damnation (apoleia — destruction) slumbereth not (is not asleep)' — the false teachers driven by greed, using deceptive, plausible words and false promises to exploit believers for profit (treating the flock as merchandise to be sold for gain, the cover of greed with fair speech); their judgment, though long ago appointed, not idle or delayed but awake and ready, certain and impending though it may seem to tarry (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: through covetousness shall they with feigned words make merchandise of you
whose judgment now of a long time lingereth not, and their damnation slumbereth not
the covetous false teachers whose certain judgment does not slumber
greed exploiting the flock under impending doom

schools.summary: The first example proving certain judgment: 'for if God spared not (did not exempt from punishment) the angels that sinned, but cast them down to hell (tartarosas — consigned them to Tartarus, the abyss/gloomy underworld), and delivered them into chains (or pits, seirois/seirais) of darkness, to be reserved (kept, held) unto judgment' — the first of three examples (angels, the flood, Sodom) proving God's certain judgment on the wicked; even angels, exalted as they were, not spared when they sinned but cast into the gloomy abyss and bound in darkness, held in confinement awaiting the final judgment (their present custody not their final doom but a holding until the great day) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: if God spared not the angels that sinned, but cast them down to hell
delivered them into chains of darkness, to be reserved unto judgment
God not sparing the sinning angels, reserved for judgment
the first proof of certain judgment

schools.summary: The second example: 'and spared not the old world (the antediluvian world), but saved Noah the eighth (person) (Noah with seven others, eight in all), a preacher (kerux — herald) of righteousness, bringing in the flood (kataklysmos — the deluge) upon the world of the ungodly' — God not sparing the wicked ancient world yet preserving Noah and his family (judgment on the many, salvation for the few); Noah a preacher of righteousness who proclaimed righteousness to the doomed world, warning them while the ark was preparing (the righteous witness amid the ungodly); the ungodly world destroyed by the flood, God's judgment falling on the wicked and sparing only the righteous (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness
bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly
God sparing not the old world but saving Noah in the flood
judgment on the wicked, deliverance for the righteous

schools.summary: The third example: 'and turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow (katastrophe — utter destruction), making them an ensample (hypodeigma — example, warning) unto those that after should live ungodly' — God reducing Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, condemning them by total overthrow (destroyed by fire from heaven for their gross wickedness, the completeness of the judgment); the destruction standing as a perpetual example and warning to all future ungodly people, their doom a sign of what awaits the wicked — the third proof of certain judgment on the ungodly (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow
making them an ensample unto those that after should live ungodly
Sodom and Gomorrah made an example of judgment
a perpetual warning to the ungodly

schools.summary: The counterpart to the judgment: 'and delivered just (righteous) Lot, vexed (kataponoumenon — distressed, worn down, oppressed) with the filthy conversation (the licentious conduct, behaviour in lasciviousness) of the wicked' — amid the destruction of Sodom, God rescuing righteous Lot, who had been grieved and distressed by the immoral conduct of the people around him; Lot called 'just' (righteous) though he dwelt in Sodom, his deliverance showing that God knows how to rescue the godly even from the midst of the wicked; the daily sight of their lawless immorality grieving his righteous soul (the godly pained, not pleased, by surrounding wickedness) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked
the deliverance of righteous Lot, distressed by the wicked
God rescuing the godly from the midst of the wicked
the righteous soul grieved by surrounding immorality

schools.summary: The explanation of Lot's vexation: '(for that righteous man dwelling among them, in seeing and hearing, vexed (ebasanizen — tormented, tortured) his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful (lawless) deeds)' — living among the wicked, Lot daily tormented in his righteous soul by what he saw and heard of their lawless conduct; his torment coming 'in seeing and hearing' (the unavoidable daily exposure to the wickedness of Sodom, the sights and sounds of their sin he could not escape, paining him continually); 'from day to day' a continual, daily anguish, the righteous man unable to share or approve the surrounding sin suffering inwardly because of it (the mark of a godly soul being grief over the sin it must witness) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: that righteous man dwelling among them... vexed his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful deeds
Lot's righteous soul daily tormented by the unlawful deeds around him
the inward suffering of the godly amid the wicked
grief over witnessed sin the mark of a righteous soul

schools.summary: The conclusion drawn from the three examples: 'the Lord knoweth how (has the wisdom and power) to deliver the godly out of temptations (peirasmou — trials, testings), and to reserve (keep, hold) the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished' — the examples of Noah and Lot proving that God knows how to rescue the godly from amid trials and the surrounding wicked (deliverance assured for the righteous, God able to preserve his people even from the most corrupt and dangerous surroundings, so they need not fear); and the same God who delivers the godly keeping the wicked in custody, reserved for punishment at the final judgment (the angels, the old world, and Sodom proving the unjust are held for their doom) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations
to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished
the Lord delivers the godly and reserves the unjust for judgment
rescue for the righteous, custody for the wicked

schools.summary: The description of those reserved especially for judgment: 'but chiefly them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness (defiling, sensual desire), and despise government (kyriotetos — lordship, authority, dominion). Presumptuous (tolmetai — daring, audacious) are they, selfwilled (authadeis — arrogant, self-pleasing), they are not afraid to speak evil of (blaspheme, revile) dignities (doxas — glories, exalted beings or authorities)' — the false teachers marked above all by sensuality (fleshly lust) and by contempt for authority (rejecting and scorning all rightful authority, whether civil, ecclesiastical, or divine, lawlessness their character); their boldness going so far as to revile dignities, the exalted ones (whether angelic powers or earthly authorities), their arrogance knowing no reverence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness, and despise government
Presumptuous are they, selfwilled, they are not afraid to speak evil of dignities
the chief offenders: the sensual and the despisers of authority
sensuality, lawlessness, and arrogant reviling

schools.summary: The comparison shaming the false teachers: 'whereas angels, which are greater (than these false teachers) in power and might, bring not railing accusation (blasphemon krisin — a reviling, blasphemous judgment) against them (the dignities, or the false teachers themselves) before the Lord' — even the holy angels, far greater than the arrogant false teachers, not bringing reviling charges (their superior power matched by reverent restraint); the angels vastly greater in power and might yet refraining from the railing the false teachers freely indulge, leaving judgment to God (cf. Jude 9, Michael not daring to bring a railing accusation against the devil), their restraint before the Lord rebuking the reckless blasphemy of the false teachers who dare what even angels will not (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: angels, which are greater in power and might, bring not railing accusation against them before the Lord
even angels do not rail against dignities
the angels' reverent restraint shaming the revilers
leaving judgment to God

schools.summary: The degrading comparison: 'but these, as natural brute beasts (aloga zoa — irrational animals, governed by instinct), made (born) to be taken (captured) and destroyed, speak evil of (blaspheme, revile) the things that they understand not; and shall utterly perish (be destroyed) in their own corruption (phthora — destruction)' — the false teachers like unreasoning animals, creatures of mere instinct born only to be caught and killed (living by appetite not reason); they revile what they do not comprehend (their blasphemy springing from ignorance, railing against truths beyond their grasp); and like the beasts to which they are likened, they will perish, their own corruption the ruin that destroys them, the doom fitting the brutish life (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: these, as natural brute beasts, made to be taken and destroyed, speak evil of the things that they understand not
shall utterly perish in their own corruption
the false teachers like brute beasts, perishing in their corruption
ignorant blasphemy and certain ruin

schools.summary: Their shameless excess and defilement: 'and shall receive the reward (wages) of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot (tryphen — revel, indulge in luxury) in the day time. Spots (spiloi — stains) they are and blemishes (momoi — disgraces), sporting themselves (reveling) with their own deceivings (apatais — deceptions, or luxuries) while they feast with you' — paid the wages of their unrighteousness; people who delight in daytime revelry (openly indulging in dissipation without even the cover of night); blots and disgraces on the Christian community (far from adorning the church, defiling and dishonouring it); reveling in their deceptions even as they share the believers' feasts (the agape love-feasts), turning sacred fellowship into self-indulgence, their presence corrupting the very feasts of charity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: shall receive the reward of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot in the day time
Spots they are and blemishes, sporting themselves with their own deceivings while they feast with you
the false teachers as spots and blemishes, reveling shamelessly
corrupting the love-feasts with self-indulgence

schools.summary: The depth of their corruption: 'having eyes full of adultery (literally, full of an adulteress — eyes that see every woman as an object of lust), and that cannot cease from sin; beguiling (deleazontes — enticing, luring, baiting) unstable (asteriktous — ungrounded, unsteady) souls: an heart they have exercised (gegymnasmenen — trained, disciplined) with covetous practices; cursed children (children of the curse)' — their very eyes saturated with lust and incapable of ceasing from sin (sin their settled, unbreakable habit); preying on those not firmly grounded in the faith (luring the weak and unstable into their errors and vices); their hearts practiced experts in covetousness; and they 'cursed children,' under God's curse and destined for condemnation (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: having eyes full of adultery, and that cannot cease from sin; beguiling unstable souls
an heart they have exercised with covetous practices; cursed children
the false teachers full of lust and greed, beguiling the unstable
practiced sinners under the curse

schools.summary: The apostasy and type: 'which have forsaken (abandoned) the right (straight) way, and are gone astray (wandered off), following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor (Beor), who loved the wages of unrighteousness' — the false teachers having abandoned the straight path of truth and righteousness and wandered into error (deliberate departure from the right way); imitating Balaam, the Old Testament prophet who for reward sought to curse Israel and led them into sin (Numbers 22-24), the type of the religious teacher corrupted by greed; Balaam loving the wages/reward of unrighteousness (the money offered him for wrongdoing, greed for gain his ruin as it is the false teachers', the love of ill-gotten reward marking them both) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: which have forsaken the right way, and are gone astray, following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor
who loved the wages of unrighteousness
the false teachers following the greedy way of Balaam
greed for gain the common ruin

schools.summary: The account (Numbers 22): 'but was rebuked (reproved) for his iniquity (transgression): the dumb ass (donkey) speaking with man's voice forbad (restrained, hindered) the madness (paraphronian — insane folly) of the prophet' — Balaam rebuked for his sin by his own donkey, which God enabled to speak with a human voice, checking the prophet's mad course; the wonder being that God made the speechless animal speak (a striking rebuke that a dumb beast should reprove the prophet, the lower creature correcting the higher, shaming Balaam's folly); Balaam's covetous course called 'madness' (an irrational folly), that it took a speaking donkey to check him underscoring how far greed had blinded him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: was rebuked for his iniquity: the dumb ass speaking with man's voice forbad the madness of the prophet
Balaam rebuked by the dumb donkey for his madness
a dumb beast reproving the covetous prophet
greed an irrational folly

schools.summary: The figures of emptiness and the doom: 'these are wells (springs) without water, clouds that are carried (driven) with a tempest (storm, whirlwind); to whom the mist (zophos — blackness, gloom) of darkness is reserved (kept) for ever' — the false teachers promising much but giving nothing, like a spring that has no water or a cloud that brings no rain but is driven off by the storm (empty and disappointing); a 'well without water' deceiving the thirsty traveler who comes expecting refreshment and finds none (so they promise spiritual truth and life but yield nothing, their show empty); and the blackest darkness reserved for them eternally, their empty deception ending in everlasting gloom, the doom of the deceivers (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: these are wells without water, clouds that are carried with a tempest
to whom the mist of darkness is reserved for ever
the false teachers as empty wells and waterless clouds, reserved for darkness
promising refreshment but yielding none

schools.summary: Their seductive method: 'for when they speak great swelling (huperonka — bombastic, pompous, overblown) words of vanity (emptiness), they allure (deleazousin — entice, bait) through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness (lasciviousness), those that were clean (just now, barely) escaped from them who live in error' — the false teachers uttering pompous, high-sounding but empty words (impressive in form, hollow in substance); luring their victims by appealing to fleshly desires, baiting them with sensual indulgence (the bait the gratification of lust); and targeting recent converts who had just escaped from the pagan life of error, seeking to lure back into corruption those newly delivered from it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: when they speak great swelling words of vanity, they allure through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness
those that were clean escaped from them who live in error
the false teachers luring back through fleshly lusts those newly escaped
pompous empty words baiting new converts

schools.summary: The hollow promise and its irony: 'while they promise them liberty (freedom), they themselves are the servants (douloi — slaves) of corruption (phthora — moral depravity): for of whom (whatever) a man is overcome (mastered, conquered), of the same is he brought in bondage (enslaved)' — the false teachers offering freedom (likely freedom from moral restraint, antinomian license) yet themselves enslaved to corruption (cannot give what they do not have); those who promise liberty themselves slaves, bound by their own lusts, their 'freedom' the worst slavery, the bondage of sin; the principle being that whatever masters a man enslaves him (the one conquered by his lusts the slave of his lusts), so the false teachers, overcome by corruption, are its bondservants and would enslave others under the name of freedom (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: while they promise them liberty, they themselves are the servants of corruption
of whom a man is overcome, of the same is he brought in bondage
the false teachers promising liberty while themselves slaves of corruption
whatever masters a man enslaves him

schools.summary: The case described: 'for if after they have escaped (fled from) the pollutions (miasmata — defilements) of the world through the knowledge (epignosis) of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, they are again entangled therein, and overcome (mastered), the latter end is worse with them than the beginning' — these had once escaped the world's corruption through the knowledge of Christ (an apparent conversion, a real outward reformation), but having escaped are again caught and conquered by the corruption they had left (the relapse into the old sins); their final state worse than their first, the relapse after knowledge more guilty and more ruinous than the original heathen state — to have known and forsaken the way worse than never to have known it (cf. Matthew 12:45, the last state worse than the first) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: if after they have escaped the pollutions of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, they are again entangled therein
the latter end is worse with them than the beginning
the worse end of those who relapse after escaping
the relapse more ruinous than the original corruption

schools.summary: The principle of aggravated guilt: 'for it had been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness, than, after they have known it, to turn (turn back) from the holy commandment delivered unto them' — to know the way of righteousness (the gospel, the Christian path) and then forsake it being worse than never to have known it (knowledge bringing greater responsibility and greater guilt in apostasy); knowledge of the way increasing accountability (the one who knew and turned away sinning against light, more guilty than the ignorant); to turn from the holy commandment (the gospel rule of life) delivered to them being deliberate apostasy, a turning away from the known truth and rule, this rejection of received light the height of their guilt (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: it had been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness
than, after they have known it, to turn from the holy commandment delivered unto them
it were better never to have known the way than to turn from it
apostasy from known truth the worse condemnation

schools.summary: The two proverbs closing the chapter: 'but it is happened unto them according to the true proverb, The dog is turned to (returns to) his own vomit again (Proverbs 26:11); and the sow that was washed to her wallowing in the mire' — the relapsed apostate like a dog returning to its vomit (taking up again what it had cast off, the disgusting picture of returning to forsaken sin) and like a washed sow returning to the mud; the dog returning because its nature is unchanged, and the washed sow returning to the mire because, though cleaned outwardly, its nature loves the mire — both proverbs exposing that the apostate's outward reformation never changed the inward nature (outwardly cleansed but never inwardly renewed), the return to sin revealing a heart that was never truly changed (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: The dog is turned to his own vomit again; and the sow that was washed to her wallowing in the mire
the two proverbs picturing the relapse into corruption
outward washing without inward renewal
the relapse revealing an unchanged heart
